name: Capital World
terrain: Dense urban sprawl dominates every Capital World — multi-story buildings provide garrison positions with interlocking fields of fire, wide avenues funnel vehicles into kill zones while offering assault corridors, elevated overpasses and mag-rail bridges create critical height advantage positions, and underground tunnel networks allow infantry flanking routes beneath the surface. Destructible environments are key: buildings collapse under sustained fire, creating rubble cover that reshapes the battlefield mid-engagement. Expect fortified government districts, industrial sectors with heavy machinery cover, civilian zones with narrow alleys, and orbital defense battery emplacements that can be captured and turned against attackers.
visual: From orbit, a Capital World blazes with light — city grids cover 40%+ of the surface in geometric patterns of civilization. Orbital rings of defense platforms, shipyards, and commercial stations form a visible halo. The atmosphere carries a permanent industrial haze, punctuated by the glow of massive energy reactors. Approaching fleets see a world wrapped in the infrastructure of empire — comm-sat constellations, debris fields from centuries of orbital construction, and the ever-present patrol routes of defense flotillas.
special: Always faction-controlled at campaign start — never spawns neutral or contested. Territory count scales by faction power level (The Revenant: 8 fixed territories with +40% structural defense bonus reflecting their architectural mastery; Unity Accord: 18 base territories expandable through diplomatic integration). Losing a Capital World triggers a faction-wide morale crisis. Orbital bombardment is possible but destroys infrastructure permanently, reducing post-capture yield by 50%. Siege warfare can last 20+ cycles.
narrative: The beating heart of a civilization. Assaulting a Capital World is not a battle — it is a CAMPAIGN. Sixteen to twenty-two territories of increasingly fortified urban terrain where every city block is a fortress, every intersection is a kill zone, and every underground passage hides defenders who know every centimeter of their home. Capitals don't fall to brute force. They fall to attrition, cunning, and the willingness to pay the price in blood. The faction that holds the most Capital Worlds holds the galaxy.

name: Terrestrial
terrain: The most tactically balanced terrain in the galaxy — open grasslands provide clean sightlines for ranged engagements, scattered forests offer light cover and concealment for ambush tactics, river crossings create natural chokepoints that reward defensive positioning, and rolling hills provide moderate height advantage without the extreme verticality of mountain worlds. Mixed terrain forces diverse army compositions: pure infantry struggles in open fields, pure vehicles bog down in forests, and balanced forces dominate. Weather patterns shift between clear skies (favoring ranged), rain (reducing visibility to 60m), and fog banks (infantry ambush conditions).
visual: Earth-like beauty from orbit — deep blue oceans covering 60-70% of the surface, green and brown continental landmasses with visible mountain ranges, white cloud formations tracking weather systems. These are the worlds every faction recognizes as 'home' regardless of origin. Scattered city lights on the night side reveal moderate population density. Visible agricultural patterns on temperate continents.
special: 40% random encounter chance per cycle — the galaxy's most contested real estate draws attention from everyone. Balanced resource yield makes them universally valuable: no faction can afford to ignore them, no faction gains disproportionate advantage from holding them. Terran League doctrine was literally designed for Terrestrial warfare. The 'standard' planet against which all other combat modifiers are measured — baseline encounters, baseline terrain, baseline everything.
narrative: Control the majority of Terrestrial worlds and you control the galactic economy. Every faction wants them. Every faction fights to hold them. They are the backbone of any empire — not the most dramatic battlefields, but the ones that decide who wins the war. Terrestrial campaigns are wars of attrition: slow, grinding, and ultimately determined by who has better logistics and more reserves.

name: Volcanic
terrain: A hellscape of obsidian ridges providing hard cover that shatters under heavy weapons fire, active lava flows creating impassable channels that reshape the battlefield as eruptions redirect molten rivers, volcanic vents that pulse with 3% HP AOE damage every 10 seconds in a 15m radius, and ash clouds that reduce accuracy by 10% beyond 50m during eruption events. The ground itself is unstable — seismic tremors can collapse tunnel systems and shift terrain elevation mid-battle. Infantry without environmental sealing take continuous atmospheric damage from sulfur and heat. Vehicles with sealed hulls operate normally but risk lava flow entrapment if positioned carelessly.
visual: An angry world of orange-red surface glow visible from deep space — active lava flows trace luminous arterial patterns across the dark volcanic surface. Mountain chains of active volcanoes belch columns of ash into a thick, choking atmosphere. The sky is a permanent twilight of sulfurous haze punctuated by the red glow of eruptions. From orbit, the planet pulses like a burning coal, its surface a constantly shifting mosaic of cooling obsidian and fresh magma.
special: 20% chance of Rich Veins trait (doubles Scrap yield to a devastating 10⚙/cycle). Highest pure Scrap income of any planet type, but zero Intel and zero Influence — a resource world, not a cultural or intelligence hub. Scrap-Horde units are environmentally adapted: immune to atmospheric damage, +15% combat effectiveness in volcanic terrain. The Horde doesn't just tolerate this environment — they were forged in it. Hazardous Atmosphere trait is guaranteed: biological units without sealed equipment lose 0.5% HP per minute of exposure.
narrative: Resource-rich but brutally hostile. Volcanic worlds are the forge-worlds of the galaxy — mountains of raw material locked behind environments that kill the unprepared. Perfect for factions that thrive in harsh conditions, and a death sentence for those that don't. The Scrap-Horde treats Volcanic worlds the way other factions treat Terrestrial ones: as home. Every other faction sees a hostile extraction site. The Horde sees paradise.

name: Ocean/Archipelago
terrain: Island warfare defines every engagement — small landmasses connected by bridges, causeways, and shallow fords create a patchwork of defensible positions separated by impassable water. Amphibious assault capability is required for island-to-island movement; factions without dedicated transport units cannot project power across water gaps. Beach chokepoints funnel attackers into pre-sighted kill zones. Dense tropical interiors provide concealment once past the beaches. Periodic tidal flooding events submerge low-lying territories for 30-second intervals, forcing units to higher ground or drowning infantry caught in the open.
visual: A water world — 90%+ ocean surface with scattered island chains and atolls visible from orbit like scattered emeralds on deep blue silk. Planet-spanning tropical storm systems track across the ocean, their spiral arms covering entire hemispheres. Shallow reefs create turquoise halos around island groups. The few settlements cluster on the largest islands, their structures built to withstand the constant storms. From orbit, the planet appears almost entirely blue, broken only by the white of cloud systems and the dark green of island vegetation.
special: 15% chance of Flux Deposit trait (+3 Intel per cycle — deep ocean currents carry exotic particles useful for intelligence gathering). Air superiority is absolutely critical: no land-based anti-air installations can cover the water gaps between islands, making air units the dominant force projection tool. Transport units become the most strategically valuable asset — controlling who can move between islands determines who controls the planet. Unity Accord naval doctrine gives them significant advantage. Eternal Shards' crystal-resonance technology allows limited teleportation across water gaps.
narrative: Ocean worlds force a fundamentally different style of warfare. Island-hopping campaigns, amphibious assaults, and air dominance battles replace the ground-based attrition of continental warfare. Factions without transport capacity cannot project power across the water gaps — they are trapped on whatever island they land on, watching helplessly as enemies island-hop around them. The ocean is not empty space; it is an impassable wall that only the prepared can cross.

name: Jungle
terrain: Multi-layered canopy warfare — the 30m visibility limit turns every engagement into a close-quarters nightmare. Vine-covered ruins from pre-spaceflight civilizations provide fortifiable positions with ancient stone walls. River crossings create natural ambush points where the canopy thins enough for limited air support. Hostile indigenous fauna adds an unpredictable third combatant to every engagement. All non-infantry units suffer -15% movement speed as roots, vines, and soft ground impede mechanical locomotion. The canopy blocks orbital reconnaissance, making fog-of-war absolute until ground units physically enter a territory.
visual: From orbit, an unbroken emerald canopy stretches from pole to pole — the jungle is so dense that no ground is visible from space. Massive river systems thread silver paths through the endless green, the only landmarks in an ocean of vegetation. Frequent tropical storms dump torrential rain that feeds the explosive growth. At night, bioluminescent organisms create ghostly blue-green light patterns visible from low orbit. The atmosphere is thick with moisture, creating a permanent haze layer that softens the world's edges.
special: Highest encounter rate of any standard planet type at 50% — the galaxy's most dangerous exploration environment. 25% chance of Indigenous Species event (primitive but hostile natives). 20% chance hostile fauna appears in tactical battles as a neutral third force. Infantry-heavy armies excel in the close quarters; vehicle-heavy armies struggle with mobility penalties. Scrap-Horde melee units thrive in the visibility-limited environment. Eternal Shards suffer: their ranged crystal-lance doctrine is severely hampered by 30m sightlines. Zero Influence yield — the jungle doesn't care about politics.
narrative: Every territory assault is an expedition into hostile terrain. The environment is as dangerous as the enemy — and the enemy knows how to use it. Jungle worlds consume armies that aren't prepared for them. Vehicles rust in the humidity, electronics short in the rain, and the canopy swallows orbital surveillance like a green black hole. The factions that master jungle warfare gain access to Intel-rich worlds that others cannot hold. Those that don't learn to fight in the green hell simply disappear into it.

name: Desert
terrain: Flat sand plains provide minimal cover but excellent vehicle mobility — this is tank country. Rocky mesas rise abruptly from the desert floor, offering commanding height advantage positions that are nearly impossible to assault from below. Deep canyons create natural chokepoints where a few defenders can hold against many. Sandstorms sweep across the battlefield in 30-second intervals, reducing accuracy by 20% and providing concealment for repositioning. Vehicle-friendly flat terrain means armored forces dominate the open ground between terrain features. Infantry caught in the open are extremely vulnerable to ranged fire.
visual: A world of harsh beauty — endless sand seas ripple to the horizon under a white-hot sun, interrupted by massive rocky mesas that cast long shadows across the dunes. Dry canyon systems cut deep into the sandstone, their walls layered in millions of years of geological history. No surface water exists. The atmosphere is crystal-clear between storms, offering unlimited visibility, then drops to near-zero when the sand walls hit. At sunset, the entire world turns gold and red.
special: 15% chance of Buried Cache trait (one-time salvage of 10-20⚙ from pre-collapse supply depots buried under the sand). Ranged combat dominance: Terran League Hammerhead Hover-Tanks excel in the long sightlines, while melee-focused units struggle crossing hundreds of meters of open ground. Scrap-Horde assault doctrine is at a severe disadvantage unless they can close distance using sandstorm concealment. Water logistics affect garrison maintenance — holding desert territories costs +10% upkeep for biological units. The Revenant is immune to water requirements.
narrative: Desert warfare is about MOBILITY and POSITION. Units in the open are dead. Controlling mesas and canyon mouths determines who wins — and who dies in the sand. The desert strips warfare to its essentials: move fast, shoot far, hold the high ground. Factions that bring the right tools dominate. Factions that don't find their armies bleaching in the sun.

name: Ice World
terrain: Cracking frozen lakes create lethal environmental hazards — vehicles have a 10% fall-through chance per crossing, taking 15% HP damage and a 5-second immobilization as they struggle free. Snow drifts reduce infantry speed by 10% and make tracked movement unpredictable. Ice caves provide underground flanking routes but risk collapse under artillery bombardment. Blizzard events reduce visibility to 15m for 20-second intervals, turning ranged engagements into point-blank ambushes. The cold itself is a weapon: equipment degrades 5% faster between battles, increasing maintenance costs for prolonged campaigns.
visual: Continental ice sheets stretch from pole to pole, broken only by glacial mountain ranges that thrust jagged peaks through the frozen surface. Frozen oceans show stress fractures visible from orbit as dark lines across white expanses. The thin, clear atmosphere between storms reveals brilliant polar auroras dancing across the sky. During blizzards, the entire world disappears under swirling white. At the equator, brief summer thaws create temporary liquid water that refreezes into treacherous thin-ice zones.
special: 20% chance of Cryo-Vault trait (preserved ancient technology locked in ice — can provide tech tree shortcuts or unique equipment). The hostile environment degrades all equipment between battles, increasing maintenance costs by 15% for sustained campaigns. The Revenant units are completely immune to cold effects — no movement penalties, no equipment degradation, no HP loss from exposure. This gives them an enormous strategic advantage on Ice Worlds, making these planets de facto Revenant territory in the mid-game. Eternal Shards crystal structures become brittle in extreme cold, suffering +10% damage from kinetic weapons.
narrative: Ice Worlds test logistics as much as tactics. The cold is a second enemy that never stops fighting — it degrades equipment, slows movement, kills exposed infantry, and makes every battle more expensive than the last. Campaigns here are wars of endurance: whoever can sustain their forces longer wins, regardless of tactical skill. The Revenant's immunity to cold makes them the undisputed masters of frozen warfare. Everyone else is just visiting.

name: Ancient Ruins
terrain: Massive megastructures from a civilization that predates all current factions by millennia — 10m-thick walls that shrug off conventional weapons, vehicle-width corridors that channel movement into predictable paths, underground chamber networks that extend for kilometers. Partially functional systems add chaos: active energy fields that damage anything passing through, collapsed sections that create impassable rubble zones, and dormant automated defenses that activate unpredictably and attack BOTH sides. The architecture defies conventional military doctrine — rooms are sized for beings far larger than any current species.
visual: From orbit, the remains of an impossibly advanced civilization scar the surface — continental-scale geometric patterns etched into the landscape by engineering beyond current comprehension. Some structures still glow with residual power after thousands of years. The atmosphere is artificially maintained by systems that no living faction understands. At ground level, the scale is overwhelming: doorways 20 meters tall, corridors wide enough for armored columns, and machinery whose purpose can only be guessed at. Holographic glyphs still flicker in the darkness.
special: ALWAYS contain lore fragments — the primary resource for the Unification victory path. 30% chance of Precursor Tech Cache (game-changing technology shortcuts). Highest encounter rate at 70% — these worlds attract every faction, pirate group, and independent scavenger in the sector. Critical for Guardian diplomacy: the only locations where Guardian negotiation thresholds can be improved through lore discoveries. The Revenant considers them sacred heritage sites and will prioritize their capture above almost all other objectives. Fighting inside the ruins negates most conventional advantages — vehicle superiority means nothing in corridors.
narrative: The galaxy's libraries, museums, and mysteries — all locked behind the most dangerous exploration environments in known space. Ancient Ruins are the primary source of lore fragments required for the Unifier ending path, making them strategically essential for any faction pursuing that victory condition. But the 70% encounter rate means you're never alone in the ruins. Every faction, every scavenger, every opportunist in the sector is drawn to these worlds. The ruins don't just contain knowledge — they attract conflict like a magnet attracts iron.

name: Gas Giant
terrain: Orbital platform warfare — all combat takes place on artificial structures suspended in the upper atmosphere of a world with no solid surface. Enclosed walkways limit movement to narrow corridors, open bridges expose units to high winds that push lightweight units toward lethal edges. Units knocked off platforms are destroyed instantly — they fall into a crushing atmosphere with no recovery. Wind shear on exposed platforms pushes units 2m per second toward edges during storm events. All cover is constructed, not natural: barricades, bulkheads, storage containers. No terrain elevation exists except between platform levels.
visual: A massive banded atmosphere dominates the view — swirling bands of amber, cream, and rust stretch across a world hundreds of times larger than any terrestrial planet. No solid surface exists. Multiple moons orbit in visible proximity. Planet-spanning storm systems larger than entire terrestrial worlds churn in the atmosphere. Mining platforms and orbital stations appear as tiny specks against the enormous backdrop, connected by thread-thin transit lines. The view from a platform edge is vertigo made real: an infinite fall into crushing pressure and darkness.
special: Cannot be colonized traditionally — only orbital platforms and atmospheric mining rigs provide habitable space. Combat is cramped, dangerous, and claustrophobic: the exact opposite of desert warfare. Scrap-Horde melee units excel in the tight corridors. Heavy vehicles cannot be deployed on most platforms (weight limits). Lighter, faster units are paradoxically more vulnerable due to wind-push effects near platform edges. Gas Giant mining provides excellent Scrap yield but nothing else — pure industrial extraction.
narrative: Fighting on atmospheric platforms is a nightmare of tight corridors, lethal drops, and the constant awareness that the floor beneath you is a pressurized lid over an infinite abyss. One wrong step, one well-placed explosion near an edge, and units simply cease to exist — swallowed by an atmosphere that would crush a starship. No one fights for Gas Giants because they want to. They fight because the resources are too valuable to ignore and too dangerous to extract peacefully.

name: Moon
terrain: Low gravity fundamentally changes combat physics — +20% movement speed for all units as reduced gravity allows longer strides and faster acceleration. Projectile arcs flatten significantly, extending effective range by 15-20% for ballistic weapons. Craters of all sizes provide natural cover bowls — units inside craters are protected from direct fire but vulnerable to plunging attacks. No atmosphere means no sound: visual cues replace audio warnings, making ambushes more effective. Vacuum exposure deals 0.5% HP per minute to unsealed infantry. Dust kicked up by movement hangs in the airless environment for extended periods, creating visual obscuration.
visual: A small airless body scarred by eons of impact craters — from sharp-rimmed fresh impacts to ancient erosion-smoothed basins. Low-gravity dust plains catch and reflect the light of the parent planet, creating an ethereal silver-gray landscape. Without atmosphere, shadows are absolute black against blinding white sunlit surfaces. The parent planet dominates the sky, a massive presence that serves as a constant directional reference. Bootprints and vehicle tracks persist indefinitely in the undisturbed regolith.
special: Low Gravity environment is guaranteed on every Moon — the physics changes are always in effect, not a random trait. Strategically valuable primarily for POSITION rather than resources: a Moon orbiting a Gas Giant provides staging access to atmospheric mining platforms. Moons orbiting Capital Worlds serve as forward operating bases for siege operations. Quick to conquer (3-5 territories) but quick to lose — low territory count means a single successful assault can flip control. 20% encounter chance keeps them from being free real estate.
narrative: Quick skirmishes on small battlefields — 3 to 5 territories that can be conquered in a single session. The resource yield is minimal, but the strategic value is everything. A Moon in the right orbit is worth more than a Terrestrial world in the wrong system. Lunar warfare is fast, violent, and decisive: there's nowhere to retreat, nowhere to hide for long, and the low gravity turns every engagement into a high-speed ballet of death.

name: Orbital Station
terrain: Interior corridor warfare at its most intense — narrow passageways barely wide enough for two units abreast, sealed bulkheads that can be locked to create chokepoints, open cargo bays that serve as the only 'wide' combat spaces, reactor rooms where stray fire risks catastrophic decompression, and maintenance shafts that allow single-file flanking routes. NO vehicles can be deployed. NO air units can operate. This is infantry-only warfare in tight, claustrophobic environments where melee contact is nearly guaranteed within the first 30 seconds of any engagement. Breaching charges on bulkheads and airlocks are the primary tactical tool.
visual: An artificial structure hanging in the void — not a world but a construction of metal, composite, and purpose. From outside, a sprawling complex of modules, docking arms, communication arrays, and solar panels. Interior spaces range from cramped maintenance corridors lit by emergency lighting to expansive command centers with viewports showing the stars. The station creaks and groans with the stress of orbital mechanics. Every surface is functional: conduits, panels, access hatches. Nothing is decorative. The vacuum of space is always one hull breach away.
special: May contain critical infrastructure: ship repair facilities that reduce fleet maintenance costs, communication arrays that extend Intel gathering range, or orbital defense weapon platforms that protect the entire system. Scrap-Horde gains +25% melee damage bonus in station environments, making them devastatingly effective in corridor combat. Unity Accord ranged doctrine is severely compromised: their carefully maintained engagement distances are impossible in 3m-wide corridors. Decompression events can occur from structural damage, instantly killing all unsealed units in affected sections. Variable yield reflects the station's function and condition.
narrative: The most intense infantry-focused engagements in the game. Every corner is a potential ambush. Every sealed door may have enemies behind it. Every wall is a potential breach point. Station warfare strips away everything except close-quarters skill — no vehicles, no air support, no terrain advantage, no long-range doctrine. Just soldiers, weapons, and corridors. Factions that excel at melee combat own stations. Factions that depend on combined arms find themselves fighting with one hand tied behind their back.

name: Dead World
terrain: Flat, featureless wasteland stretching to the horizon — scattered boulders provide the only hard cover, shallow impact craters offer slight defilade positions, and occasional ancient wreckage from whatever catastrophe killed this world creates improvised fortifications. Sightlines extend to maximum weapon range in all directions: this is the most ranged-dominant terrain in the galaxy. Infantry in the open are exposed and vulnerable. Vehicles dominate. The complete absence of terrain features means there is nowhere to hide, nowhere to ambush, and nowhere to retreat. Combat is brutal, direct, and fast.
visual: A world that died — barren, lifeless surface stretching to every horizon without a single living thing. No atmosphere to scatter light means the sky is black even during the day, stars visible alongside the local sun. No water, no vegetation, no movement except the slow drift of ancient dust. The surface is scarred by whatever killed it: impact craters, vitrified glass plains from orbital bombardment, or the slow erosion of a world that simply ran out of time. From orbit, it's a gray-brown sphere of nothing — the galaxy's tombstone.
special: 40% chance of Derelict Fleet trait (salvageable or reactivatable ships buried in the surface or orbiting as debris — can provide significant military assets if recovered). 15% chance of hazardous environmental effects (radiation zones, unstable ground, toxic residue). 60% encounter rate — surprisingly high for a worthless-looking world, because Dead Worlds near the galactic core may contain Guardian ruins and pre-collapse technology. Near-zero resource yield makes them appear worthless, but the salvage opportunities and lore potential draw scavengers and researchers from across the galaxy.
narrative: The galaxy's graveyards. Something happened here — war, ecological collapse, stellar event, or something worse. The remains tell stories to those willing to dig, and the 60% encounter rate proves that plenty of factions are willing to dig. Dead Worlds are the ultimate risk-reward proposition: terrible resource yield, dangerous environmental hazards, but the chance of finding a Derelict Fleet or Guardian ruins makes them worth the gamble. Every Dead World is a mystery. Not all mysteries have pleasant answers.
